text: This video demonstrates a robotic assisted appendectomy for acute appendicitis with moderate to severe cecal inflammation. With this degree of cecal inflammation, a simple appendectomy procedure may not be sufficient for this patient. Conservative surgical recommendations might include ileocecectomy. Depending on the surgeon's comfort level with minimally invasive techniques, this may require conversion from a laparoscopic to an open approach. This video demonstrates an alternative option, that is, an omental gram patch applied to the appendiceal stump. This has been previously described in the literature, albeit through an open technique.

This patient presented with a three-day history of right lower quadrant pain. CT scan demonstrated a non-perforated appendicitis with extensive cecal inflammation. Given the patient's physical exam, I was not comfortable with the non-operative approach of antibiotics and bowel rest. We therefore proceeded to the operating room for robotic assisted appendectomy and all indicated procedures.

Three trocars are placed in a vertical line along the left rectus abdominis muscle, with the center incision at the umbilicus and additional incisions above and below. The inflamed cecum is visible, complicating the appendectomy procedure, as traditional suturing or stapling methods are deemed unsuitable due to the risk of tissue damage.

Conservative recommendations in such a case could include ileocecectomy. As mentioned before, this might require an open operation depending on the surgeon's comfort level with robotic or laparoscopic techniques. In addition, sacrificing the ileocecal valve can have substantial consequences for the patient. Depending on age and other comorbidities, this could range from slight irritation to incontinence and dehydration. I chose instead to perform an omental gram patch over the appendiceal stump to reinforce the closure. This procedure appears underwhelming at first glance, but the comorbidities of a larger resection can have a cascading effect. A larger resection, such as an ileosecectomy, could negatively impact the patient's lifestyle. In addition, an ileocecctomy may require a moderate midline incision. This results in a longer hospital stay, increased postoperative pain, larger risk of superficial or deep infection, and a higher risk of incisional hernia.

The surgeon uses interrupted vicryl sutures to reinforce the closure of the appendiceal stump, preserving the ileocecal valve. Two O-silk sutures are placed at the superior and inferior base of the stump, tying them over the omental patch to approximate the omentum to the inflamed stump.

This patient was discharged home on post-operative day one. Pathology was consistent with a standard appendicitis. In follow-up clinic after two weeks, the patient had a full recovery and was symptom free. It is also worth noting that as this operation does not require a stapler, it costs less than a standard laparoscopic appendectomy.
I hope these videos have been helpful. Thank you for watching.
